items.prompt: Theory of Mind (ToM) typically develops around:
choices: At the moment of birth with full understanding of others' mental states immediately present
Age 4-5, as demonstrated by passing false-belief tasks like the Sally-Anne test scenario
Around age 15 during the formal operational stage of adolescent cognitive development
Only in adulthood when prefrontal cortical executive functioning reaches complete neurological maturation
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) ToM: understanding that others have different beliefs, desires, and knowledge than oneself. False-belief tasks: child must predict another's behavior based on the OTHER person's (false) belief, not reality. Most children pass by age 4-5. Delayed in autism spectrum disorder.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Infants lack explicit ToM (though some implicit understanding exists earlier).
(C) ToM develops in preschool years, not in adolescence.
(D) ToM typically develops in early childhood, well before adulthood.

items.prompt: Retroactive interference occurs when:
choices: Old information interferes with learning new information
New information interferes with recall of old information
Both old and new information are equally forgotten
Information decays over time without interference
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Retroactive interference = NEW learning disrupts recall of OLDER information. E.g., learning a new phone number makes it harder to recall your old one.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Old information disrupting new learning = proactive interference (the opposite direction)
(C) Equal forgetting of both doesn't describe a specific interference pattern
(D) Time-based decay is a different theory of forgetting (trace decay theory), not interference
directReferences: Müller, G. E., & Pilzecker, A. (1900). Experimentelle Beiträge zur Lehre vom Gedächtnis [Experimental contributions to the science of memory]. Zeitschrift für Psychologie, Ergänzungsband 1, 1–300.

items.prompt: The Zeigarnik effect refers to:
choices: Systematically forgetting formally successfully completed routine required tasks
Better memory for INCOMPLETE or interrupted tasks than for completed ones
Consistently meticulously always finishing required specific tasks to completion
Significant robust memory improvement occurring exclusively primarily during sleep
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Zeigarnik (1927): uncompleted tasks create a state of cognitive tension that keeps them more accessible in memory. Once completed, the tension resolves and the memory becomes less accessible.

Why others are wrong:
(A) COMPLETED tasks are actually remembered LESS well than interrupted ones
(C) The effect is about MEMORY for interruptions, not always completing tasks
(D) Sleep-related memory consolidation is a separate phenomenon
directReferences: Zeigarnik, B. (1927). Das Behalten erledigter und unerledigter Handlungen [The retention of completed and uncompleted activities]. Psychologische Forschung, 9, 1–85.

items.prompt: The testing effect (retrieval practice) demonstrates that:
choices: Testing only functions strictly as an objective standardized measurement of baseline learning
Actively retrieving information from memory strengthens retention MORE than additional studying
Participating in frequent repeated testing actually systematically permanently impairs memory
Only utilizing highly specific standardized multiple-choice tests can broadly improve memory
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Roediger & Karpicke: the act of RETRIEVING information from memory (testing) strengthens memory traces more than re-reading or re-studying. This is one of the most robust and practically important findings in cognitive psychology.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Testing doesn't just MEASURE — it ENHANCES learning (a direct learning tool)
(C) Testing STRENGTHENS memory, not impairs it
(D) Multiple formats (free recall, short answer, flashcards) produce the testing effect — not just multiple-choice
directReferences: Roediger, H. L., & Karpicke, J. D. (2006). Test-enhanced learning: Taking memory tests improves long-term retention. Psychological Science, 17(3), 249–255. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2006.01693.x

items.prompt: Source monitoring errors occur when:
choices: People perfectly accurately flawlessly identify exactly where they learned semantic information
People fundamentally misattribute the specific original SOURCE or context of a stored memory
Exclusively only young developing children frequently consistently make these specific errors
Information sources are universally always accurately strictly correctly correctly identified
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Source monitoring (Johnson et al.): errors in identifying WHERE or HOW information was acquired. Examples: false memories from imagination inflation, cryptomnesia (unconscious plagiarism), confusing dreams with reality.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Source monitoring errors specifically involve IMPERFECT source identification
(C) Adults also make source monitoring errors — they're especially common in older adults and certain clinical conditions
(D) Source misattribution is common and well-documented
directReferences: Johnson, M. K., Hashtroudi, S., & Lindsay, D. S. (1993). Source monitoring. Psychological Bulletin, 114(1), 3–28. https://doi.org/10.1037/0033-2909.114.1.3

items.prompt: The availability heuristic leads people to:
choices: Judge probability based on how easily examples come to mind
Anchor estimates to initial values
Ignore base rate information
Seek information that confirms existing beliefs
items.rationale:
Availability heuristic (Tversky & Kahneman): judging frequency/probability by how readily instances come to mind. Dramatic events (plane crashes) seem more common than they are.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Seeking confirming evidence = confirmation bias, a different heuristic/bias
(C) Anchoring to initial values = anchoring heuristic, a different judgment bias
(D) Preferring the status quo = status quo bias, unrelated to the availability heuristic
directReferences: Tversky, A., & Kahneman, D. (1973). Availability: A heuristic for judging frequency and probability. Cognitive Psychology, 5(2), 207–232. https://doi.org/10.1016/0010-0285(73)90033-9

items.prompt: The Zeigarnik effect refers to:
choices: Significantly better memory retrieval for successfully completed cognitive tasks
Better memory for incomplete or interrupted tasks compared to completed ones
Rapid forgetting of information due to proactive or retroactive cognitive interference
Global memory improvement exclusively following restorative slow-wave sleep cycles
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Zeigarnik (1927): interrupted or incomplete tasks are better remembered than completed ones. The tension of incompletion maintains cognitive engagement. Applied in cliffhangers, study strategies, and understanding intrusive thoughts in OCD.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Completed tasks are actually remembered WORSE (the tension is resolved)
(C) Interference → proactive/retroactive interference
(D) Sleep-dependent memory consolidation is a different phenomenon
directReferences: Zeigarnik, B. (1927). Das Behalten erledigter und unerledigter Handlungen [The retention of completed and uncompleted activities]. Psychologische Forschung, 9, 1–85.

items.domainName: Social and cultural bases of behavior
items.prompt: Perspective-taking (empathic accuracy) involves:
choices: Only seeing things from your own viewpoint
Cognitively understanding another person's thoughts, feelings, and viewpoint
Always agreeing with others
Ignoring others' experiences
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Perspective-taking: the cognitive component of empathy — mentally simulating another's experience. It reduces prejudice, increases prosocial behavior, and improves interpersonal relationships. Distinct from emotional empathy (feeling others' emotions).

Why others are wrong:
(A) Only seeing your own viewpoint = egocentrism, the opposite of perspective-taking
(C) Understanding another's perspective doesn't require agreeing with them
(D) Perspective-taking specifically involves ENGAGING with others' experiences, not ignoring them
directReferences: Batson, C. D. (2011). Altruism in humans. Oxford University Press.

items.domainName: Social and cultural bases of behavior
items.prompt: Groupthink (Janis) is MORE likely when:
choices: The group encourages dissent
The group is highly cohesive, isolated from outside opinions, has a directive leader, and faces high stress
Group members are strangers
There is no time pressure
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Janis: antecedents of groupthink include high cohesion, insulation, lack of methodical procedures, directive leadership, homogeneous background, and high stress with low hope of alternatives. Examples: Bay of Pigs, Challenger disaster.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Encouraging dissent PREVENTS groupthink — it's a key remedy
(C) High COHESION (not stranger status) contributes to groupthink
(D) High stress and time pressure INCREASE groupthink by promoting premature consensus
directReferences: Janis, I. L. (1982). Groupthink: Psychological studies of policy decisions and fiascoes (2nd ed.). Houghton Mifflin.

items.domainName: Social and cultural bases of behavior
items.prompt: The Robbers Cave experiment (Sherif) demonstrated:
choices: That intergroup conflict cannot be resolved
Competition for scarce resources created intergroup hostility, and SUPERORDINATE GOALS (requiring cooperation) reduced conflict
That prejudice is entirely innate
That separation always reduces conflict
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Sherif et al. (1961): Phase 1 — in-group formation. Phase 2 — intergroup competition created intense hostility. Phase 3 — superordinate goals (common problems requiring cooperation) reduced conflict. Foundation for realistic conflict theory.

Why others are wrong:
(A) The study showed conflict CAN be resolved through superordinate goals
(C) The study showed prejudice arising from competitive SITUATIONS, not innate disposition
(D) Mere separation didn't reduce conflict — cooperative interdependence was needed
directReferences: Sherif, M., Harvey, O. J., White, B. J., Hood, W. R., & Sherif, C. W. (1961). Intergroup conflict and cooperation: The Robbers Cave experiment. University of Oklahoma Press.

items.domainName: Social and cultural bases of behavior
items.prompt: The contact hypothesis (Allport) states that intergroup prejudice is reduced when:
choices: Groups simply co-exist near each other
Contact occurs under OPTIMAL conditions: equal status, common goals, intergroup cooperation, and support of authorities/institutions
All contact automatically reduces prejudice
Separation between groups is maintained
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Allport (1954): mere contact is insufficient — sometimes it increases prejudice. Four conditions optimize contact: equal status between groups, common goals, cooperative interdependence, and institutional support/social norms favoring equality.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Mere proximity (co-existence) can actually INCREASE prejudice without optimal conditions
(C) Contact WITHOUT Allport's conditions can worsen prejudice
(D) Separation prevents the intergroup contact needed to reduce prejudice
directReferences: Allport, G. W. (1954). The nature of prejudice. Addison-Wesley.

items.domainName: Social and cultural bases of behavior
items.prompt: The Stanford Prison Experiment (Zimbardo) demonstrated:
choices: That personality alone determines behavior
The POWER OF SITUATIONAL FORCES — normal individuals assigned to guard/prisoner roles quickly adopted oppressive/submissive behaviors
That prisons have no effect on behavior
Only criminals behave aggressively
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Zimbardo (1971): psychologically healthy college students randomly assigned as guards exhibited cruelty and domination; prisoners became passive, anxious, and distressed. Demonstrates situational power over behavior. (Note: methodological criticisms have been raised.)

Why others are wrong:
(A) The study showed SITUATIONAL forces overwhelmed personality factors
(C) The prison simulation powerfully affected behavior — that was the key finding
(D) The guards were normal, screened participants — not criminals — yet they behaved aggressively
directReferences: Haney, C., Banks, W. C., & Zimbardo, P. G. (1973). Interpersonal dynamics in a simulated prison. International Journal of Criminology and Penology, 1, 69–97.

items.domainName: Social and cultural bases of behavior
items.prompt: Right-wing authoritarianism (RWA, Altemeyer) is characterized by:
choices: Only economic conservatism
High conventionalism (adherence to established norms), authoritarian submission (deference to authority), and authoritarian aggression (hostility toward norm violators)
Only left-wing ideology
Social liberalism
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Altemeyer's three components: (1) conventionalism (strong adherence to traditional norms), (2) authoritarian submission (uncritical deference to established authorities), and (3) authoritarian aggression (aggression toward people perceived as violating norms).

Why others are wrong:
(A) RWA is a personality/attitudinal construct, not purely economic
(C) Despite the name, RWA can be found across political spectrums (it's about submission to authority)
(D) High RWA is associated with social conservatism, not liberalism
directReferences: Altemeyer, B. (1996). The authoritarian specter. Harvard University Press.

items.domainName: Social and cultural bases of behavior
items.prompt: The looking-glass self (Cooley) proposes that:
choices: Self-concept is entirely innate
Our self-concept is formed by how we IMAGINE others perceive us — we see ourselves as we think others see us
Self-concept has nothing to do with social interaction
Only objective self-evaluation matters
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Cooley (1902): three components — (1) we imagine how we appear to others, (2) we imagine their judgment of our appearance, (3) we develop feelings about ourselves based on our perception of their judgments. Self is fundamentally social.

Why others are wrong:
(A) The looking-glass self theory emphasizes that self-concept is SOCIALLY constructed
(C) Social interaction is CENTRAL to this theory — self-concept IS a social product
(D) The theory emphasizes PERCEIVED (imagined) judgments, which may differ from objective reality
directReferences: Cooley, C. H. (1902). Human nature and the social order. Scribner.

items.domainName: Social and cultural bases of behavior
items.prompt: Leon Festinger's cognitive dissonance theory predicts that a person who is paid $1 (vs. $20) to lie about a boring task will:
choices: Report the task as more boring
Report the task as more enjoyable
Show no change in attitude
Refuse to participate in the study
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) The $1 group lacks sufficient external justification for lying, creating more dissonance. To reduce dissonance, they change their attitude to believe the task was actually enjoyable. The $20 group has sufficient external justification and experiences less dissonance.

Why others are wrong:
(A) They'd only find it MORE boring if dissonance drove them to contrast their experience with the lie
(C) The insufficient justification ($1) specifically produces attitude change — this is the study's key finding
(D) Festinger & Carlsmith's participants did not refuse; they participated and showed predictable attitude changes

items.domainName: Social and cultural bases of behavior
items.prompt: Microsaggressions are:
choices: Extremely overt and systematically structured physical acts of systemic discrimination
Brief, commonplace exchanges that communicate derogatory messages to marginalized groups
Explicitly intentional and formally verifiable severe hate crimes conveying prejudice
Explicitly constructed continuous explicit verbal insults routinely given beyond context
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Microaggressions (Sue, 2007) are subtle, often unintentional slights — verbal, behavioral, or environmental — that communicate hostile or derogatory messages to members of marginalized groups.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Overt discrimination = macro-level or explicit; microaggressions are subtle and often unconscious
(C) Hate crimes are criminal acts; microaggressions are everyday interactions, usually not criminal
(D) Microaggressions can be behavioral (clutching a purse), environmental (only White faces in marketing), or verbal — not just verbal insults

items.prompt: Cross-sectional research designs compare:
choices: The same individuals over time
Different age groups at one point in time
Identical twins only
Laboratory vs. field settings
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Cross-sectional designs compare different cohorts (age groups) simultaneously. They're faster and cheaper than longitudinal but can't separate age effects from cohort effects (generational differences).

Why others are wrong:
(A) Same individuals over time = longitudinal design
(C) Twin studies are a behavioral genetics methodology, not defined by cross-sectional vs. longitudinal
(D) Lab vs. field = a methodological distinction about setting, not about cross-sectional design

items.prompt: Selective attention improves significantly during:
choices: Early infancy (0-2 years old)
Middle childhood (6-12 years)
Exclusively during adulthood
The sensitive prenatal period
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Selective attention — focusing on relevant information while filtering irrelevant — shows major improvement during middle childhood. This is partly why formal schooling begins around this age and why ADHD symptoms become more apparent in school.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Infant attention is primarily stimulus-driven and involuntary
(C) Development begins in childhood, not only adulthood
(D) Prenatal development doesn't involve attention as measured

items.prompt: Object permanence typically develops around:
choices: Immediately precisely following postnatal early infancy biological emergence and birth
8-12 months of age (coinciding directly with Piaget's specific sensorimotor substage 4)
Around chronological age 5 marking late early childhood cognitive developmental maturity
Late chronological adolescence approaching young adult developmental cognitive functioning
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Object permanence: understanding that objects continue to exist when out of sight. Develops around 8-12 months. Before this, 'out of sight, out of mind.' Piaget's substage 4 — the infant begins searching for hidden objects.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Not present at birth — develops gradually
(C) Age 5 is well past this milestone
(D) Object permanence is an infancy achievement

items.prompt: The bystander effect (Darley & Latané) refers to:
choices: People always immediately help in every emergency situation regardless of surrounding circumstances
Decreased likelihood of helping as the number of bystanders increases, from diffusion of responsibility
Bystanders invariably intervene in emergencies with greater frequency when more witnesses are present
The phenomenon occurring only in densely populated urban geographical areas and never suburban settings
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Bystander effect: more witnesses → less individual helping. Mechanisms: diffusion of responsibility ('someone else will help'), pluralistic ignorance (looking at others' inaction and concluding no emergency), evaluation apprehension (fear of embarrassment). Kitty Genovese case sparked research.

Why others are wrong:
(A) The effect demonstrates specifically reduced helping with more bystanders.
(C) The opposite is demonstrated — more bystanders leads to less individual intervention.
(D) The effect has been replicated across diverse settings and geographical contexts.

items.prompt: Obedience research by Milgram found that:
choices: Virtually nobody obeys destructive authority commands under any experimental research conditions
Approximately 65% of participants administered maximum 'shock' when instructed by an authority figure
Only clinically diagnosable psychopaths demonstrate obedience to destructive authority figure commands
Destructive obedience to authority occurs exclusively within formal military organizational chain-of-command structures
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Milgram's obedience studies: ~65% of participants went to the maximum 450-volt level. Factors increasing obedience: proximity to authority, distance from victim, institutional setting, incremental escalation, no model of disobedience. Demonstrated the power of situational forces.

Why others are wrong:
(A) The surprising finding was the remarkably HIGH rate of obedience.
(C) Normal, psychologically well-adjusted people demonstrated obedience.
(D) Obedience to authority occurs across many diverse organizational contexts.

items.prompt: Cognitive dissonance theory (Festinger) predicts that insufficient justification leads to:
choices: No measurable attitude change regardless of the magnitude of justification provided externally
Greater attitude change — small external justification drives internal attribution and belief shifts
Complete psychological apathy and total disengagement from the counter-attitudinal behavior performed
Only observable behavioral change without any corresponding shift in underlying cognitive attitude
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Insufficient justification: when people perform a counter-attitudinal behavior for a small reward (insufficient external justification), they experience dissonance and change their attitude to match the behavior. Classic: $1 condition → more attitude change than $20 condition.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Dissonance theory specifically and successfully predicts attitude change.
(C) Dissonance creates active motivation to reduce the cognitive inconsistency.
(D) Attitude change is the primary theoretical prediction, not just behavioral change.

items.prompt: The contact hypothesis (Allport) states that prejudice can be reduced when groups:
choices: Compete directly against each other for scarce resources in zero-sum intergroup rivalry contexts
Have equal status, pursue common goals, cooperate interdependently, and have institutional support
Simply exist in geographical proximity without any structured interaction or shared goals
Deliberately and systematically avoid all forms of intergroup contact and social interaction
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Allport's conditions for prejudice reduction through contact: (1) equal status in the situation, (2) common goals, (3) cooperative interaction (not competitive), (4) support from authorities/institutions. Meta-analyses confirm these conditions optimize prejudice reduction.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Competition can actually INCREASE prejudice (Sherif's Robbers Cave experiment).
(C) Geographical proximity alone is insufficient — specific conditions are required.
(D) Systematically avoiding contact maintains and reinforces existing stereotypes.

items.prompt: Elaboration Likelihood Model (Petty & Cacioppo) describes two routes to persuasion:
choices: Both processing routes are fundamentally identical in their cognitive mechanisms and outcomes
Central route (deep argument processing) and peripheral route (relying on surface cues)
Only one single cognitive processing route to persuasion exists within the theoretical framework
Neither processing route involves any form of cognitive elaboration or information processing
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Central route: high motivation and ability → careful scrutiny of argument quality → durable attitude change. Peripheral route: low motivation/ability → influenced by heuristics and cues (expert source, attractive speaker) → less durable change. Personal relevance increases central processing.

Why others are wrong:
(A) The routes involve fundamentally different levels of cognitive processing.
(C) Two distinct persuasion processing routes are specifically proposed.
(D) Both routes involve cognitive processing but at qualitatively different levels.

items.prompt: Just-world hypothesis (Lerner) leads people to:
choices: Consistently help victims more because of heightened empathy for their unjust suffering
Blame victims for their misfortune to maintain the belief that the world is fundamentally fair
Actively and persistently seek true justice through institutional reform and social advocacy
Never attribute blame to anyone regardless of factual circumstances or behavioral contributions
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Just-world belief: 'people get what they deserve.' When confronted with innocent suffering, this belief is threatened. People may victim-blame to restore the belief. Implications: blaming sexual assault victims, dismissing poverty as laziness, attributing illness to character.

Why others are wrong:
(A) The just-world hypothesis often REDUCES helping through victim-blaming.
(C) People maintain the illusion of justice through cognitive distortions, not reforms.
(D) The bias specifically leads to attributing blame to innocent victims.

items.prompt: Collectivist cultures, compared to individualist cultures, tend to emphasize:
choices: Personal achievement and individual accomplishment above all other social and relational values
Group harmony, interdependence, loyalty to in-group, and fitting in rather than standing out
Only interpersonal competition and individual dominance over peers in social hierarchies
Individual rights, personal autonomy, and self-expression above all collective social obligations
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Collectivism: the self is defined in relation to others (interdependent self-construal). Values: group harmony, reciprocity, saving face, filial piety, duty. Why others are wrong:

(C) Individual competition characterizes individualist cultural values.
(D) Individual rights emphasis defines individualist cultures specifically.

items.prompt: On the WAIS-IV, the Perceptual Reasoning Index (PRI) is composed of which three core subtests?
choices: Block Design, Matrix Reasoning, Visual Puzzles
Similarities, Vocabulary, Information
Digit Span, Arithmetic, Letter-Number Sequencing
Symbol Search, Coding, Cancellation
items.rationale:
The PRI on the WAIS-IV measures nonverbal fluid reasoning, spatial processing, and visual-motor integration. Its three core subtests are Block Design (spatial relations, visual-motor), Matrix Reasoning (analogical fluid intelligence, culture-fair), and Visual Puzzles (mental, non-motor spatial rotation).

Why others are wrong:
• Similarities, Vocabulary, Information are the Verbal Comprehension Index (VCI).
• Digit Span, Arithmetic, Letter-Number Sequencing (optional) are the Working Memory Index (WMI).
• Symbol Search, Coding, Cancellation (optional) are the Processing Speed Index (PSI).

items.prompt: According to Beck's Cognitive Model, the 'cognitive triad' of depression consists of negative views about:
choices: The past, the present, and the future
The self, the world (environment), and the future
Id, ego, and superego
Thoughts, feelings, and behaviors
items.rationale:
Aaron Beck proposed that depression is maintained by a negative cognitive triad: (1) negative views of the self ('I am worthless'), (2) negative views of the world/environment ('Everything is unfair and hostile'), and (3) negative views of the future ('Things will never get better').

Why others are wrong:
• A focus on past/present/future is too broad; Beck specifically targeted the self, the environment, and the future as the foci of depressive ideation.
• Id, ego, superego are Freudian structural concepts.
• Thoughts, feelings, and behaviors are the interactive triangle of CBT generally, but not the specific 'triad of depression.'

items.prompt: Bipolar I disorder is distinguished from Bipolar II disorder primarily by:
choices: The severity and duration of the depressive episodes
The presence of psychotic symptoms during depression
The occurrence of a full manic episode vs. only hypomanic episodes
The rapid cycling between moods
items.rationale:
The defining feature of Bipolar I is the occurrence of at least one full manic episode (lasting at least 1 week, causing marked impairment to functioning, or requiring hospitalization). Bipolar II requires at least one major depressive episode AND at least one hypomanic episode (lasting at least 4 days, noticeable by others, but NOT causing severe impairment or requiring hospitalization). If there is ever a full manic episode, the diagnosis automatically becomes Bipolar I.

Why others are wrong:
• Depressive episodes are required for Bipolar II, but their severity alone doesn't differentiate it from Bipolar I (which also has severe depressions).
• Psychotic symptoms can occur in severe depression in both disorders; however, psychosis during a 'high' automatically qualifies it as full mania (Bipolar I).
• Rapid cycling (4+ episodes per year) is a specifier that can be applied to either type.

items.prompt: Actuarial (statistical) prediction differs from clinical prediction in that it:
choices: Relies on subjective clinical judgment
Uses empirically derived formulas and algorithms to make predictions
Is always less accurate
Doesn't use data
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Actuarial prediction uses statistical formulas combining predictor variables with empirically established weights. Research consistently shows actuarial methods are equal to or better than clinical judgment for most prediction tasks.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Subjective judgment = clinical prediction, not actuarial
(C) Actuarial methods are generally MORE accurate than clinical judgment (Meehl, 1954; Grove et al., 2000)
(D) Actuarial methods are heavily data-driven — they rely entirely on empirical data and statistical relationships

items.prompt: Prosopagnosia is:
choices: A severe expressive language impairment significantly hindering verbal communication capabilities
The inability to recognize familiar faces, typically resulting from damage to the fusiform face area (fusiform gyrus) in the temporal lobe
A generalized comprehensive progressive systemic neurodegenerative declarative memory disorder
An essential neurodevelopmental movement disorder disrupting voluntary muscular somatic control
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Prosopagnosia: 'face blindness.' Acquired (brain damage) or developmental (congenital). Fusiform face area (FFA) in the right fusiform gyrus is specialized for face processing. Patients may recognize people by voice, clothing, or context. Can still identify objects.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Language → aphasia
(C) It's a visual recognition deficit, not a general memory disorder
(D) Movement disorders → basal ganglia/cerebellar conditions

items.prompt: The Stroop Test measures:
choices: Exclusively evaluating baseline rudimentary fundamental reading processing speed acquisition metrics
Selective attention and response inhibition — the ability to suppress automatic responses (reading) in favor of a controlled response (naming ink color)
Primarily mapping pure sensory physiological peripheral color vision perceptual threshold capacities
Focusing entirely upon measuring long-term structural semantic declarative memory encoding skills
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Stroop: name the ink color of color words (e.g., 'RED' printed in blue ink → say 'blue'). The Stroop effect (slower for incongruent trials) reflects the automaticity of reading and the cognitive control needed to inhibit it. Sensitive to frontal lobe dysfunction.

Why others are wrong:
(A) The Stroop involves reading suppression, not reading speed
(C) It's about cognitive control, not just color perception
(D) The Stroop measures attention/inhibition, not memory

items.prompt: Face validity refers to:
choices: A scientifically rigorous validity measure
Whether a test APPEARS to measure what it's supposed to measure — based on surface-level examination, NOT empirical evidence
The same as construct validity
Only expert judgment
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Face validity: subjective assessment of whether a test LOOKS like it measures the intended construct. It's the WEAKEST form of validity — not based on empirical evidence. However, it affects test-taker motivation and public acceptance.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Face validity is NOT scientifically rigorous — it's a subjective, surface-level judgment
(C) Construct validity requires empirical evidence (convergent, discriminant); face validity is just appearance
(D) Expert judgment contributes to CONTENT validity — face validity is a layperson's impression
directReferences: Anastasi, A., & Urbina, S. (1997). Psychological testing (7th ed.). Prentice Hall.

items.prompt: Incremental validity asks:
choices: Is the test reliable?
Does the test ADD predictive value BEYOND what existing measures already provide?
How many items should the test have?
Is the test culturally fair?
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Incremental validity: does adding this test to the existing assessment battery significantly improve prediction? If existing measures already capture the relevant variance, a new test may not add value despite being valid on its own.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Reliability is a separate question
(C) Item number relates to test design, not incremental validity
(D) Cultural fairness is a test bias issue

items.prompt: Stimulus generalization occurs when:
choices: A specific conditioned response reliably occurs only upon exposure to the single original stimulus
A conditioned response readily occurs upon exposure to novel stimuli SIMILAR to the original CS
The previously established conditioned response is completely extinguished through repeated trials
No measurable physiological or behavioral psychological response reliably occurs at all
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Stimulus generalization: the CR occurs to stimuli resembling the CS. Little Albert (Watson): fear conditioned to a white rat generalized to other furry objects (rabbit, fur coat, Santa beard). Gradient: more similar stimuli elicit stronger responses.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Response only to the original stimulus = stimulus discrimination
(C) Extinction is the weakening of the CR through unreinforced presentations of the CS
(D) Generalization produces responses, not absent responses

Only passively reading theoretical psychoeducational materials covering optimal social skills

items.rationale:
Correct: (B) SST components: psychoeducation (instruction), modeling (demonstrating the skill), behavioral rehearsal (role-playing), feedback (from therapist and group), and homework (real-world practice). Used for social anxiety, schizophrenia, ASD, ADHD.

Why others are wrong:
(A) SST is a psychosocial intervention
(C) Reading alone is insufficient — practice is essential
(D) The goal is to approach, not avoid, social situations

items.prompt: Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) for PTSD involves:
choices: Only bilateral eye movements performed in isolation without any structured therapeutic processing protocol
An 8-phase protocol using bilateral stimulation while processing traumatic memories adaptively
Only progressive muscle relaxation techniques applied without targeting specific traumatic memories
Classical hypnotic trance induction procedures designed to access deeply repressed traumatic material
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) EMDR (Shapiro): 8 phases including history-taking, preparation, assessment (target memory + negative cognition + desired positive cognition), desensitization (bilateral stimulation), installation, body scan, closure, and reevaluation.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Eye movements are one form of bilateral stimulation; tapping and auditory tones also work.
(C) Relaxation may be part of preparation but isn't the active treatment component.
(D) EMDR is a distinct evidence-based protocol, fundamentally different from hypnosis.

items.prompt: Behavioral activation (BA) for depression is based on:
choices: Cognitive restructuring techniques targeting distorted automatic thoughts and maladaptive core beliefs
The principle that depression involves reduced positive reinforcement, and increasing valued activities restores it
Classical psychoanalytic dream interpretation techniques used to uncover unconscious conflict material
Free association methods allowing uncensored verbal expression of unconscious thoughts and impulses
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) BA (Lewinsohn → Martell, Addis): depression involves behavioral withdrawal → fewer positive reinforcers → more depression. BA systematically schedules meaningful activities aligned with values, uses activity monitoring, and addresses avoidance patterns.

Why others are wrong:
(A) BA specifically focuses on behavioral activation, not cognitive restructuring (though both combine in CBT).
(C)(D) BA is a behavioral intervention, not a psychodynamic approach.

items.prompt: Systematic desensitization (Wolpe) combines:
choices: Extensive sustained unstructured flooding exposing clients directly toward overwhelmingly distressing environmental stimuli
Progressive relaxation with a hierarchy of feared stimuli, working from least to most anxiety-provoking through reciprocal inhibition
Exclusively utilizing highly targeted fast-acting anxiolytic medication explicitly regulating severe physiological panic
Primarily focusing upon rational cognitive therapy systematically dismantling conscious catastrophic ideation patterns
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Systematic desensitization: (1) Learn deep relaxation, (2) Construct a fear hierarchy (SUDs 0-100), (3) Pair relaxation with each hierarchy item, moving up as anxiety diminishes. Reciprocal inhibition: relaxation and anxiety can't coexist. Gradual counterconditioning.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Flooding involves immediate, prolonged exposure to the most feared stimulus — no hierarchy
(C) Medication is not part of systematic desensitization
(D) It's a behavioral technique based on classical conditioning principles

items.prompt: Transtheoretical Model stages of change (Prochaska & DiClemente) include:
choices: Strictly exclusively solely identifying exactly two functional strictly linear specific fixed stages
Precontemplation, contemplation, preparation, action, and maintenance, with recycling across stages
Strictly exclusively explicitly recognizing only specific action and related maintenance set stages
Strictly exclusively solely identifying exactly one single comprehensive continuous uniform stage
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) TTM stages: Precontemplation (no intention to change), Contemplation (ambivalent, considering change), Preparation (planning to change), Action (actively changing), Maintenance (sustaining change). Relapse can occur at any point → recycling. Implications: match interventions to stage.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Five stages are identified
(C) Three preceding stages are equally important
(D) Five distinct stages are proposed

items.prompt: Paradoxical intention (Frankl) involves:
choices: Systematically precisely thoroughly explicitly gradually approaching previously intensely feared specific situations
Prescribing the symptom — encouraging the client to intentionally engage in or exaggerate the problematic behavior
Explicitly invariably unconditionally formally repeatedly distinctly agreeing entirely consistently with the client
Exclusively formally specifically strictly using empirical medication to intentionally produce direct opposite effects
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Paradoxical intention: asking a client who can't sleep to try to STAY AWAKE, or a person with performance anxiety to try to be AS ANXIOUS AS POSSIBLE. The paradox often reduces the symptom by removing the struggle against it.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Gradual approach = systematic desensitization
(C) Agreement is not the core mechanism
(D) Paradoxical intention is psychological, not pharmacological
directReferences: Frankl, V. E. (1967). Psychotherapy and existentialism: Selected papers on logotherapy. Simon & Schuster.

items.prompt: Structural family therapy (Minuchin) focuses on:
choices: Only the identified patient's individual intrapsychic pathology without considering family system dynamics
Reorganizing family structure — boundaries, hierarchies, and subsystems — for healthier interactions
Only unconscious dream content analyzed through psychoanalytic interpretation within family contexts
Only the marital dyad's romantic relationship issues without addressing broader family system patterns
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Structural family therapy: maps family organization (enmeshed vs. disengaged boundaries, parent-child hierarchies, coalitions, triangulations). Uses enactment, boundary making, unbalancing, and restructuring to change dysfunctional patterns.

Why others are wrong:
(A) The identified patient's symptoms reflect broader structural family problems.
(C) Dream analysis belongs to psychoanalytic tradition, not structural family therapy.
(D) Structural therapy targets the entire family system architecture, not just couples.
directReferences: Minuchin, S. (1974). Families and family therapy. Harvard University Press.

items.prompt: Paradoxical intention (Frankl) involves:
choices: Systematically avoiding the feared outcome through graduated hierarchical exposure desensitization
Intentionally trying to produce the feared symptom, which often effectively reduces driving anxiety
Completely concealing all anxiety symptoms from the treating therapist during therapeutic sessions
Only utilizing positive cognitive reframing techniques while systematically suppressing negative thoughts
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Paradoxical intention: prescribing the symptom. An insomniac is told to try to stay awake; someone with hand tremors is told to try to shake even more. By intentionally attempting the feared behavior, anticipatory anxiety is disrupted.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Systematic avoidance maintains the symptom; paradoxical intention confronts it directly.
(C) Open, transparent communication with the therapist is actively encouraged.
(D) It's about intentionally producing the feared response, not suppressing negative thoughts.
directReferences: Frankl, V. E. (1967). Psychotherapy and existentialism: Selected papers on logotherapy. Simon & Schuster.

items.prompt: In motivational interviewing, 'rolling with resistance' refers to:
choices: Directly confronting client denial
Avoiding the topic of change entirely
Acknowledging the client's perspective without arguing
Using paradoxical interventions to create resistance
items.rationale: Rolling with resistance is a core principle of motivational interviewing (Miller & Rollnick, 2002). Rather than confronting or arguing with ambivalent clients, the therapist acknowledges their perspective, reframes statements, and avoids power struggles. This approach reduces defensiveness and allows the client to explore their own motivations for change.

items.prompt: The therapeutic technique of 'empty chair' is most associated with which approach?
choices: Psychoanalytic therapy
Cognitive-behavioral therapy
Gestalt therapy
Solution-focused brief therapy
items.rationale: The empty chair technique is a hallmark of Gestalt therapy (Fritz Perls). The client engages in dialogue with an imagined person, aspect of self, or emotion seated in an empty chair. This experiential technique promotes awareness, emotional expression, and integration of polarities. It has been adapted and used in emotion-focused therapy (EFT) as well.

items.prompt: The main distinction between OCD and OCPD is:
choices: There is practically zero officially validated discrete functional clinical difference observed
OCD involves ego-dystonic obsessions/compulsions; OCPD involves ego-syntonic personality traits
OCD explicitly uniquely incorporates continuous strictly targeted pure behavioral compulsions
OCPD is objectively recognized formally documented as considerably more severe than typical OCD
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) OCD: intrusive, distressing (ego-dystonic) obsessions and compulsions the person recognizes as excessive. OCPD: pervasive pattern of orderliness, perfectionism, and control that the person views as desirable and rational (ego-syntonic).

Why others are wrong:
(A) They are distinct diagnoses with different features
(C) OCD involves both obsessions AND compulsions
(D) Severity varies independently; OCD can be extremely debilitating

items.prompt: Delirium differs from dementia primarily in:
choices: Delirium clinically completely precisely and systematically only exclusively impacts older adults
Delirium gives rapid onset and fluctuating courses; dementia has a gradual and progressive course
Dementia inherently features major key attention problems while formal delirium entirely does not
Both logically explicitly and definitively represent fundamentally the true identical conditions
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Delirium: acute onset (hours-days), fluctuating consciousness/attention, often reversible, caused by medical conditions/substances. Dementia (major neurocognitive disorder): insidious onset, progressive decline, usually irreversible, attention relatively preserved until later stages.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Delirium can occur at any age (e.g., post-surgery, high fever)
(C) The OPPOSITE — delirium primarily impairs attention
(D) They are distinct conditions requiring different interventions

items.prompt: Illness Anxiety Disorder (formerly Hypochondriasis without significant symptoms) is characterized by:

Preoccupation with having or acquiring a serious illness with minimal somatic symptoms
Actively intentionally consistently formally making up symptoms targeting external gain

Gradual social withdrawal, declining functioning, unusual perceptual experiences, and suspiciousness
Only explicitly consistent severe highly structured systematically prolonged visual hallucinations

items.prompt: The kindling model in Bipolar Disorder suggests that:
choices: Psychosocial environmental target stress remains universally formally intrinsically irrelevant to generating severe episodes
Early episodes are more likely triggered by stressors, while later episodes become increasingly autonomous and spontaneous
All distinct mood episodes invariably rigidly strictly logically and explicitly require an exogenous environmental stressor
Rigid standard pharmacological medication definitively and universally reliably absolutely continuously prevents all episodes
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Kindling hypothesis (Post): initial mood episodes are often precipitated by identifiable stressors. Over time, the brain becomes sensitized, and episodes occur with less provocation or spontaneously — like kindling a fire. This supports early intervention and maintenance treatment.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Stress IS relevant, especially to early episodes
(C) Later episodes may occur without clear stressors
(D) Medication significantly reduces but doesn't eliminate all episodes

items.prompt: The 'negative cognitive triad' (Beck) in depression consists of negative views of:
choices: The defined past, present, future
The self, the world, and a future
The defined id, ego, and superego
Thoughts, feelings, and behaviors
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Beck's cognitive triad: (1) Negative view of SELF ('I'm worthless'), (2) Negative view of the WORLD ('Nothing works out'), (3) Negative view of the FUTURE ('Things will never get better'). These automatic thoughts maintain depression.

Why others are wrong:
(A) The triad is about self, world, future — not temporal categories
(C) Id, ego, superego are psychoanalytic, not cognitive
(D) The triad is about content of cognitions, not functional components

items.prompt: Child abuse reporting is:

Mandated by law in all 50 states for psychologists
Only required if the child requests it
Only required for physical abuse, not sexual or emotional
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) All 50 states have mandatory child abuse reporting laws that include psychologists as mandated reporters. Suspected abuse must be reported — certainty is NOT required. This overrides confidentiality.

Why others are wrong:

(C) The child's request is irrelevant — the legal obligation exists regardless of the child's wishes

items.prompt: A psychologist's client reveals plans to harm a specific coworker. The psychologist should FIRST:
choices: Wait passively to see if the client acts on the stated threat over time
Assess the seriousness and imminence of the threat to the coworker
Immediately call local police to intervene and prevent potential harm
Terminate therapy immediately to avoid professional liability risks
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Before taking protective action, the psychologist must assess the credibility, specificity, and imminence of the threat. Tarasoff requires action for serious, credible threats to identifiable victims — but clinical assessment comes first to determine if the threshold is met.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Waiting passively could be negligent if the threat is serious
(C) Calling police may be warranted but only after assessing the threat — premature action violates client trust unnecessarily
(D) Termination doesn't address the safety concern

items.prompt: The duty to protect (Tarasoff) applies when:
choices: A client makes any generally vague negative hostile statement during a clinical session
A client makes a credible, specific threat against an identifiable potential victim
A client discusses an extensive detailed history of past violence and aggressive acts
A client expresses general unstructured pervasive anger toward society and institutions
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Tarasoff standard: duty to protect arises with a specific, credible, imminent threat to an identifiable third party. The duty may include warning the intended victim, notifying police, hospitalizing the client, or other protective measures.

Why others are wrong:
(A) General negative statements don't meet the Tarasoff threshold
(C) Past violence alone doesn't trigger Tarasoff (no current threat)
(D) General anger without a specific target doesn't create a duty to warn

items.prompt: A psychologist learns their client is HIV-positive and engaging in unprotected sex without disclosing to partners. The psychologist should:
choices: Immediately breach confidentiality to notify all identifiable sexual partners of the imminent medical risk
Assess the situation, encourage disclosure, and consult applicable laws regarding permissive disclosure to at-risk third parties
Maintain strict confidentiality as HIV status is legally protected health information under federal privacy regulations
Terminate therapy immediately and refer the client to an infectious disease specialist for further medical counseling
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) No universal rule exists for HIV. APA Policy: encourage client disclosure first. If the client refuses, some states allow (not require) limited disclosure to identifiable partners at risk. The psychologist must know their jurisdiction's laws and consult.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Immediately notifying without assessment or consultation is premature and potentially illegal.
(C) Confidentiality has limits; some jurisdictions provide a permissive duty to warn at-risk partners.
(D) Termination doesn't address the safety concern and constitutes abandonment.

Ensure technological competence, obtain specialized informed consent, and comply with laws in both relevant jurisdictions

Utilize any readily available commercial video platform without conducting a formal HIPAA compliance assessment
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) APA Telepsychology Guidelines require competence in the specific technology, informed consent detailing privacy risks/limitations, adherence to jurisdictional laws (where both provider and client sit), and use of secure platforms.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Licensing laws absolutely apply across state lines depending on the client's physical location.
(C) PSYPACT and temporary practice provisions ethically allow practice across state lines under strict conditions.
(D) Using unencrypted or non-HIPAA compliant platforms violates privacy and confidentiality standards.

items.prompt: Continuing education (CE) requirements required by state licensing boards exist primarily to:
choices: Generate administrative revenue for approved continuing education sponsors and state licensing agencies
Ensure psychologists systematically maintain and update their professional competence throughout their careers
Provide a mechanism to discipline or penalize psychologists who have committed prior ethical violations
Fulfill bureaucratic legislative requirements without meaningfully impacting actual clinical practice standards
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) CE requirements ensure psychologists stay current with evolving clinical science, evidence-based practice guidelines, and ethical standards. Most licensing boards require 20-40 CE hours per renewal cycle to protect the public through competence maintenance.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Revenue generation is entirely incidental to the core purpose of maintaining clinical competence.
(C) CE is proactive competence maintenance and development, not a punitive or disciplinary measure.
(D) CE serves a genuine, measurable professional development function essential for public safety.

items.prompt: Forensic psychologists tasked with conducting court-ordered evaluations must ensure examinees understand that:
choices: All disclosed information remains strictly protected by traditional doctor-patient confidentiality privilege
The evaluation does not constitute a therapeutic relationship and findings will be shared with the retaining party or court
They possess the absolute right to refuse compliance with any question without facing legal repercussions
Their participation in the evaluation is entirely voluntary regardless of the mandated context
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) APA Specialty Guidelines for Forensic Psychology: examinees must understand the nature and purpose of the evaluation, exactly who will receive the results, the significant limits of confidentiality, and that the relationship is strictly evaluative, not therapeutic.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Forensic evaluations operate with entirely different confidentiality parameters than standard psychotherapy.
(C) Refusal to participate may carry severe legal or adjudicative consequences depending on the specific court order.
(D) Court-ordered evaluations are, by definition, not voluntary.

items.prompt: The Jaffee v. Redmond (1996) Supreme Court ruling established:
choices: Mandatory reporting requirements for psychologists treating minors in federally funded programs
Federal psychotherapist-patient privilege protecting communications from compelled disclosure in court
The foundational legal insanity defense standard replacing the older M'Naghten cognitive test
The Tarasoff-based duty to warn identifiable third parties of credible imminent violent threats
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Jaffee v. Redmond: the Supreme Court recognized psychotherapist-patient privilege under Federal Rules of Evidence. Communications made during psychotherapy are confidential and cannot be compelled in federal court proceedings. Extended to licensed social workers.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Mandatory reporting requirements are established independently by individual state laws.
(C) The insanity defense has separate legal foundations (M'Naghten, ALI, GBMI).
(D) Duty to warn derives from the Tarasoff decisions, not Jaffee v. Redmond.

items.prompt: APA Standard 2.01 requires psychologists to:
choices: Practice freely in any psychological area they personally choose regardless of formal training background
Practice only within the boundaries of their competence, based on education, training, and experience
Systematically avoid all continuing education requirements after obtaining their initial doctoral licensure
Exclusively utilize only one single theoretical orientation consistently throughout their entire career
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Standard 2.01: competence is bounded by training and experience. To practice in new areas, psychologists must obtain relevant training or supervised experience. When no trained professionals are available, psychologists may provide emergency services but must seek appropriate consultation.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Psychologists must practice strictly within their documented areas of competence.
(C) Continuing education is typically required for ongoing licensure maintenance.
(D) Psychologists can ethically use multiple orientations within their competence boundaries.

items.prompt: Privileged communication differs from confidentiality in that:
choices: They are functionally identical concepts referring to the exact same professional ethical obligation
Privilege is a LEGAL concept protecting communications from forced disclosure; confidentiality is ethical
Confidentiality applies exclusively within courtroom legal proceedings during formal judicial testimony
Neither concept holds any meaningful practical importance in contemporary psychological clinical practice
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Confidentiality: an ethical duty not to share client information without consent. Privilege: a legal right that prevents forced disclosure in court/legal proceedings. The client holds the privilege (can waive it). Not all confidential information is privileged, and privilege varies by jurisdiction.

Why others are wrong:
(A) They are related but conceptually and functionally distinct obligations.
(C) Confidentiality applies across all clinical settings, not exclusively in courtrooms.
(D) Both are critically important foundational principles of professional clinical practice.

items.prompt: The concept of 'assent' in child therapy means:
choices: Full legal informed consent provided directly by the minor child participating in the treatment
The child's developmental agreement to participate in treatment, even without legal consent capacity
Only parental consent obtained from the legal guardian, without any involvement from the child
A formal court order mandating the child's participation in compulsory therapeutic intervention
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Assent: for minors who cannot legally consent, psychologists seek their agreement/willingness to participate. The child should understand (at their developmental level) what will happen, why, and their right to refuse. Legal consent comes from the parent/guardian.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Children cannot legally provide full informed consent; assent is distinct.
(C) Best practice involves obtaining both parental consent AND the child's assent.
(D) Court orders are legally separate from the clinical assent process.

items.prompt: A psychologist who receives a subpoena for client records should:
choices: Instantly unconditionally release all requested sensitive therapeutic historical documentation immediately complying fully without hesitation
Consult with an attorney, notify the client, and determine whether the subpoena is valid and whether privilege applies before releasing any information
Systematically securely meticulously destroy all requested clinical records permanently avoiding any potential subsequent legal discovery
Completely completely uniformly absolutely ignore the incoming legal document avoiding unnecessary engagement within the adversarial process
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Subpoena ≠ court order. Steps: (1) Consult an attorney, (2) Notify the client, (3) Determine if a valid authorization/waiver exists, (4) Seek to quash or limit the subpoena if appropriate, (5) Only a court order compels disclosure. Never automatically release records upon subpoena.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Immediate release violates client confidentiality and privilege
(C) Destroying records is illegal (obstruction, spoliation)
(D) Ignoring a subpoena can result in contempt of court

choices: Absolutely nothing fundamentally distinctly structurally legally strictly specialized beyond typical explicitly standardized valid regular procedures
Competence in specific technology, verifying client location, understanding inter-jurisdictional laws, maintaining functional privacy, and planning
Strictly universally explicitly narrowly firmly prioritizing entirely distinct objective administrative functional commercial financial fiscal billing
Strictly universally explicitly narrowly firmly prioritizing entirely distinct objective logistical standard functional therapeutic basic scheduling
items.rationale:

items.prompt: The duty to protect (Tarasoff) has evolved so that in most states:
choices: Strictly practically universally there is absolutely zero explicit legal professional structural standardized clinical measurable widespread overall duty
Psychologists must take reasonable steps to protect identifiable third parties when a specific client issues an objective, highly credible threat
Uniformly systematically specifically exclusively firmly reliably ensuring early direct basic objective third-party warning communication is sufficient
Uniformly systematically specifically exclusively firmly ensuring full rapid protective inpatient involuntary clinical hospitalization is required
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Tarasoff I (1974): duty to warn. Tarasoff II (1976): broadened to duty to protect — can be fulfilled through various means. State laws vary: some require warning, some require any reasonable protective action, some have no duty. Always document risk assessment and actions taken.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Most states have some form of duty to protect
(C) Warning is one option; other protective actions may also satisfy the duty
(D) Hospitalization is one option but not universally required

items.prompt: Diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) measures:
choices: Blood flow in the brain
White matter tract integrity by tracking the DIFFUSION of water molecules along myelinated axon pathways
Neurotransmitter concentrations
Brain electrical activity
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) DTI: an MRI technique that measures directional water diffusion. Water diffuses preferentially along axon bundles (anisotropic diffusion). Fractional anisotropy (FA) values indicate white matter integrity. Used to study connectivity and traumatic brain injury.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Blood flow → fMRI (BOLD signal) or arterial spin labeling
(C) Neurotransmitter concentrations → PET with specific radioligands
(D) Electrical activity → EEG or MEG
directReferences: Basser, P. J., & Pierpaoli, C. (1996). Microstructural and physiological features of tissues elucidated by quantitative-diffusion-tensor MRI. Journal of Magnetic Resonance, Series B, 111(3), 209–219. https://doi.org/10.1006/jmrb.1996.0086

items.prompt: Positron emission tomography (PET) works by:
choices: Using magnetic fields
Detecting gamma rays emitted from radioactive tracers injected into the bloodstream, revealing metabolic activity
Using ultrasound waves
Measuring skin conductance
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) PET: a radioactive tracer (e.g., fluorodeoxyglucose/FDG) is injected and metabolized by active brain cells. The tracer emits positrons → positron-electron annihilation → gamma rays detected by the scanner. Maps metabolic activity and receptor binding.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Magnetic fields → MRI/fMRI
(C) Ultrasound → transcranial Doppler or obstetric imaging
(D) Skin conductance → galvanic skin response (GSR), a peripheral measure
directReferences: Phelps, M. E. (2000). PET: The merging of biology and imaging into molecular imaging. Journal of Nuclear Medicine, 41(4), 661–681.

items.prompt: The amygdala plays a central role in:
choices: Motor coordination
Emotional processing, particularly FEAR conditioning, threat detection, and emotional memory formation
Language production
Visual acuity
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) The amygdala: rapid threat detection (subcortical 'low road' via thalamus), fear conditioning, emotional memory enhancement, social evaluation, and emotion recognition in facial expressions. Bilateral damage → Klüver-Bucy syndrome.

(A) Motor coordination → cerebellum
(C) Language production → Broca's area
(D) Visual acuity → retina and visual cortex (V1)

items.prompt: The cerebellum's role extends beyond motor coordination to include:
choices: Only balance
Cognitive functions including timing, language processing, attention, and emotional regulation
No other functions beyond motor
Only reflexes
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Modern research reveals cerebellar contributions beyond motor: cognitive timing, verbal working memory, language processing, attention shifting, and emotional regulation. Cerebellar cognitive affective syndrome documents these deficits after cerebellar damage.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Balance is ONE function — the cerebellum has extensive non-motor roles
(C) The 'motor only' view is outdated — extensive cognitive and affective functions are documented
(D) Reflexes are primarily mediated by the spinal cord — the cerebellum fine-tunes voluntary movements and supports cognition
directReferences: Schmahmann, J. D. (2019). The cerebellum and cognition. Neuroscience Letters, 688, 62–75. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neulet.2018.07.005

items.prompt: Sleep deprivation primarily affects:
choices: Only physical strength
Attention, executive function, emotional regulation, memory consolidation, immune function, and metabolic processes
Nothing significant
Only dream frequency
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Sleep deprivation impairs: sustained attention, working memory, decision-making, emotional regulation (increased amygdala reactivity), immune function (reduced NK cells), metabolism (insulin resistance), and memory consolidation (both declarative and procedural).

Why others are wrong:
(A) Sleep deprivation has WIDESPREAD cognitive, emotional, and physiological effects beyond physical strength
(C) Sleep deprivation has SIGNIFICANT, well-documented effects across multiple domains
(D) Sleep deprivation affects far more than dream frequency — it impairs waking cognition and health
directReferences: Walker, M. P. (2017). Why we sleep: Unlocking the power of sleep and dreams. Scribner.

items.prompt: The pituitary gland is called the 'master gland' because:
choices: It is the largest endocrine gland
It regulates OTHER endocrine glands through hormones like ACTH, TSH, FSH, LH, and growth hormone — though it is itself controlled by the hypothalamus
It directly produces all hormones
It has no regulatory function
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) The anterior pituitary secretes trophic hormones controlling other glands: ACTH (adrenals), TSH (thyroid), FSH/LH (gonads), GH (growth), prolactin (mammary). The posterior pituitary stores/releases oxytocin and ADH. The hypothalamus controls the pituitary.

Why others are wrong:
(A) The pituitary is actually quite small (pea-sized) — its importance is in regulatory function
(C) Many hormones are produced by peripheral glands (thyroid, adrenals, gonads) — the pituitary REGULATES them
(D) The pituitary's PRIMARY function is regulation of other endocrine glands
directReferences: Bear, M. F., Connors, B. W., & Paradiso, M. A. (2016). Neuroscience: Exploring the brain (4th ed.). Wolters Kluwer.

items.prompt: Which medication class works by blocking the reuptake of serotonin?
choices: Benzodiazepines
Tricyclic antidepressants
SSRIs
MAO inhibitors
items.rationale:
Correct: (C) Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors (SSRIs) like fluoxetine (Prozac) specifically block the serotonin transporter, increasing serotonin availability in the synapse.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Benzodiazepines enhance GABA activity at GABA-A receptors — they don't affect serotonin reuptake
(B) Tricyclics block reuptake of BOTH serotonin AND norepinephrine (less selective), plus have anticholinergic side effects
(D) MAO inhibitors prevent the enzyme monoamine oxidase from breaking down serotonin, norepinephrine, and dopamine — different mechanism than blocking reuptake

GABA
Acetylcholine
Dopamine

Why others are wrong:

(B) GABA → primary inhibitory neurotransmitter; reduces neural activity rather than signaling reward
(C) Acetylcholine → involved in memory, learning, and muscle activation, not the reward circuit

items.prompt: Seasonal Affective Disorder (SAD) has been linked to dysregulation of:
choices: Dopamine in the basal ganglia
Melatonin and circadian rhythms
Acetylcholine in the hippocampus
Endorphins in the brainstem
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) SAD is linked to disrupted circadian rhythms and abnormal melatonin secretion due to reduced daylight in winter months. Light therapy (10,000 lux) is an effective treatment that helps reset the circadian clock.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Basal ganglia dopamine dysregulation → Parkinson's disease, not SAD
(C) Hippocampal acetylcholine problems → Alzheimer's disease and memory impairment
(D) Brainstem endorphins → related to pain modulation, not seasonal mood changes

items.prompt: Teratogens are MOST dangerous during which period of prenatal development?
choices: Germinal period (weeks 1-2)
Embryonic period (weeks 3-8)
Fetal period (weeks 9-birth)
All periods are equally vulnerable
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) The embryonic period is most vulnerable because major organ systems and structures are forming (organogenesis). Teratogenic exposure during this time can cause major structural malformations.

Why others are wrong:
(A) During the germinal period, the organism is undifferentiated; exposure typically results in miscarriage rather than malformation
(C) The fetal period involves growth and refinement — teratogens can still cause damage but usually functional rather than major structural defects
(D) Vulnerability varies significantly by developmental stage and specific organ system being formed

items.prompt: Albert Ellis's Rational Emotive Behavior Therapy (REBT) identified which core irrational beliefs?
choices: All-or-nothing thinking, catastrophizing, and personalization
Demandingness, awfulizing, and low frustration tolerance
Learned helplessness and external locus of control
Negative automatic thoughts and cognitive schemas
items.rationale: Ellis identified three core irrational beliefs: demandingness (rigid 'musts' and 'shoulds'), awfulizing (catastrophizing the consequences of unmet demands), and low frustration tolerance ('I can't stand it'). These beliefs are challenged through the ABCDE model: Activating event, Beliefs, Consequences, Disputing, and new Effective philosophy.

items.prompt: The 'weapon focus effect' in eyewitness testimony demonstrates:
choices: Enhanced memory for all details during threatening events
Attentional narrowing that impairs memory for peripheral details
Superior recall by police officers
The impact of post-event information on memory
items.rationale: The weapon focus effect occurs when the presence of a weapon captures the witness's attention, drawing focus away from other details such as the perpetrator's face. This attentional narrowing results in better memory for the weapon but poorer memory for other aspects of the event, reducing the accuracy of eyewitness identification.

items.prompt: The phi phenomenon, demonstrated by Wertheimer, is an example of:
choices: Classical conditioning
Operant conditioning
Gestalt perception
Cognitive dissonance
items.rationale: The phi phenomenon (apparent motion from sequential flashing lights) was demonstrated by Wertheimer (1912) and is foundational to Gestalt psychology. It illustrates that perception is not simply the sum of sensory inputs but involves the brain organizing stimuli into meaningful wholes — 'the whole is different from the sum of its parts.'

items.prompt: The spacing effect suggests that:
choices: Massed practice is more effective than distributed practice
Distributed study sessions produce better long-term retention than cramming
Studying in the same location always improves recall
Short study sessions are always better than long ones
items.rationale: The spacing effect (Ebbinghaus, 1885) shows that distributing study across multiple sessions over time produces superior long-term retention compared to massing the same amount of study into one session (cramming). The optimal spacing interval depends on the desired retention interval. This principle underlies spaced repetition systems like the SM-2 algorithm used in this app.

items.rationale: The unconditioned response (UCR) is an automatic, unlearned reaction to the unconditioned stimulus (e.g., salivation in response to food). No learning is required for the UCR to occur.

items.domainName: Social and cultural bases of behavior
items.prompt: Collectivistic cultures tend to prioritize:
choices: Individual achievement and personal goals
Group harmony, interdependence, and collective well-being
Competition between individuals
Personal autonomy above all else
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Collectivistic cultures (East Asian, Latin American, African) emphasize group goals, social harmony, interdependence, and defined roles within the social hierarchy.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Individual achievement and personal goals are prioritized in INDIVIDUALISTIC cultures (US, Western Europe)
(C) Individual competition is more characteristic of individualistic cultures
(D) Personal autonomy is valued more highly in individualistic cultures — collectivistic cultures balance autonomy with group needs

items.prompt: Individualistic cultures tend to emphasize:
choices: Group harmony above personal goals
Personal achievement, autonomy, and individual rights
Hierarchical social structures as primary
Interdependence and collective responsibility

Why others are wrong:
(A) Group harmony = collectivistic culture value
(C) Hierarchical structures are emphasized in high power-distance cultures, which crosscuts individualism-collectivism

items.domainName: Social and cultural bases of behavior
items.prompt: Groupthink (Janis) is characterized by:
choices: Careful systematic consideration of all available alternative solutions before reaching final decisions
Pressure for unanimity that suppresses critical thinking, dissent, and independent evaluation
Productive constructive conflict and vigorous debate among all group members about decisions
Independent individual decision-making processes without any group conformity or social influence
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Janis's groupthink: cohesive groups under stress suppress dissent, create illusion of unanimity, self-censor, and use mindguards — producing defective decision-making (e.g., Bay of Pigs, Challenger disaster).

Why others are wrong:
(A) Careful consideration of alternatives is the OPPOSITE of groupthink — groupthink suppresses this.
(C) Productive conflict is suppressed in groupthink — dissent is discouraged.
(D) Groupthink is specifically a GROUP phenomenon, not individual decision-making.

items.domainName: Social and cultural bases of behavior
items.prompt: The elaboration likelihood model (Petty & Cacioppo) distinguishes between:
choices: Conscious explicit attitudes and unconscious implicit attitudes measured by different assessment tools
Central (systematic argument processing) and peripheral (heuristic cue-based) persuasion routes
Only positive favorable attitudes and negative unfavorable attitudes toward persuasion targets
Intrinsic autonomous motivation and extrinsic controlled motivation as described by self-determination
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) ELM: central route = careful evaluation of message arguments (when motivated and able), producing durable attitude change. Peripheral route = reliance on cues (attractiveness, expertise) with less lasting change.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Conscious/unconscious attitudes relate to explicit/implicit attitude research, not ELM.
(C) The ELM addresses HOW attitudes change (processing route), not attitude valence.
(D) Intrinsic/extrinsic motivation = self-determination theory (Deci & Ryan), not ELM.

items.domainName: Social and cultural bases of behavior
items.prompt: The actor-observer bias refers to:
choices: Actors attributing their own behavior to situational factors while observers attribute it to dispositional factors
Everyone within a shared cultural context making exactly the same fundamental attributional errors
Only active situational actors making consistent systematic errors in interpersonal judgment
Only passive objective observers making consistent systematic errors in interpersonal judgment
items.rationale:
Correct: (A) The actor-observer asymmetry: when explaining OUR behavior, we emphasize situations ('I was stressed'). When explaining OTHERS' behavior, we emphasize dispositions ('they're lazy'). This relates to different information available — actors see their varying situations; observers see consistent behavior.

Why others are wrong:
(B) The whole point is that actors and observers make DIFFERENT attributions
(C)/(D) Both actors and observers show biases, just in opposite directions

items.domainName: Social and cultural bases of behavior
items.prompt: Obedience in Milgram's experiments was DECREASED by:
choices: Having the primary authority figure remaining physically present in the laboratory room
Increasing the physical/psychological distance between the participant and the authority figure
Significantly making the participant the absolutely only one available for the experiment
Removing any other confederate participants who were present during the initial procedures
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Milgram's variations showed obedience decreased when: authority was remote (telephone instructions), the victim was closer (touch proximity), another 'participant' defied the authority, or the experiment was in a less prestigious setting.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Authority presence INCREASED obedience
(C) Being the only one available didn't specifically decrease obedience
(D) Confederates who defied authority decreased obedience

items.domainName: Social and cultural bases of behavior
items.prompt: Cognitive dissonance theory (Festinger) predicts that the GREATEST dissonance occurs when:
choices: Targeted acts can accurately purely clearly be attributed to a strong external justification
Freely chosen behavior contradicts an important attitude with minimal external justification
The target test participant absolutely completely fundamentally does not care about the issue
The independently documented designated experimental active behavior was safely clearly forced
items.rationale:
Correct: (B) Maximum dissonance: (1) free choice (not forced), (2) minimal external justification (can't explain it away as coerced), (3) the attitude is important. The $1/$20 experiment: $1 participants experienced more dissonance (less justification) and changed their attitudes more.

Why others are wrong:
(A) Strong external justification REDUCES dissonance (sufficient justification)
(C) Low importance = low dissonance
(D) Forced behavior reduces perceived choice, reducing dissonance
